metadata.summary: RFC 7807 Problem Details: standardize API errors with type, title, status, detail, traceId, errors[]. One shape, one content-type (application/problem+json), one framework handler.

content.title: Error Handling with RFC 7807 Problem Details
content.summary: A methodology for standardizing API error responses following RFC 7807 Problem Details: every error returns `type` (URI), `title`, `status`, `detail`, `instance`, `traceId`, and an optional `errors[]` array for field-level validation failures. All endpoints share one shape, one content-type (`application/problem+json`), and one error-handler entry point.

why.p: Inconsistent error shapes force every API client to handle each endpoint's unique format. RFC 7807 provides a machine-readable envelope that clients can parse generically, observability tools can index by `traceId`, and on-call engineers can trace across services. A single framework-level handler also prevents raw exception messages and stack traces from leaking to clients in production.

list.item: New REST API where consistent client-side error handling matters.
Refactoring mixed error shapes (`{error:"..."}`, `{detail:"..."}`, HTML) into one envelope.
Public-facing APIs where error shape is part of the published contract.
Multi-service architectures where clients must not memorize per-service formats.
API gateway / BFF setups translating downstream errors.
GraphQL — errors live in the response `errors[]` array per spec.
Internal-only low-stability APIs where the envelope overhead outweighs benefit.
Streaming endpoints (SSE, WebSocket) — error frames follow their own conventions.
Static asset / file download endpoints — return raw HTTP status, no body.

rule.statement: Every error response must include type (URI), title (short string), and status (integer matching HTTP status). detail, instance, traceId, and errors[] are optional but strongly recommended.
rule.rationale: RFC 7807 requires type, title, status as the minimum. Without them, clients cannot build generic error-handling logic.

rule.statement: Set Content-Type to application/problem+json, not application/json.
rule.rationale: RFC 7807 mandates the problem+json media type. Clients (e.g., Spring RestClient with ProblemDetail) detect typed handling via the suffix. Using application/json silently degrades to generic parsing.

rule.statement: The status field in the body must exactly match the HTTP response status code.
rule.rationale: RFC 7807 requires they match. Mismatches appear when re-wrapping downstream errors: the outer HTTP code is 502 while the body says 400.

rule.statement: Always populate traceId. Map from the W3C traceparent header if present; otherwise generate a UUID.
rule.rationale: Without traceId, supporting a customer requires log scraping by timestamp across all services. With traceId, one string resolves the incident.

rule.statement: For 5xx errors, detail must be a generic static string. Never interpolate exception messages, stack traces, SQL, internal paths, or PII.
rule.rationale: Raw exception messages leak implementation details, internal hostnames, and sometimes credentials. Provide traceId for internal lookup instead.

rule.statement: Use errors[] only for validation failures (4xx). Each item must have field, code (machine-readable slug), and message (human-readable).
rule.rationale: errors[] is a custom extension beyond RFC 7807. Using it for non-validation errors (401, 403, 404) over-structures simple responses and surprises clients expecting the spec shape.

rule.statement: Treat type URIs as part of the API contract. Once published, changing them is a breaking change.
rule.rationale: Clients may dispatch on the type URI to handle specific error classes. Renaming validation-error to validation silently breaks those clients.

rule.statement: Register a single framework-level exception handler. Handlers must not call response.json() with a shape that differs from ProblemDetail.
rule.rationale: Per-route error shapes re-create the inconsistency the methodology exists to solve. One handler, one shape, one content-type.

example.title: FastAPI ProblemDetail model and exception handler
example.note: Content-Type is set explicitly. 5xx detail is static. traceId reads from W3C traceparent header.

example.title: Contract test helper with JSON Schema
example.note: Reusable assertion helper for contract tests. Checks content-type, status range, and schema.

antipattern.description: Interpolating exception messages, stack traces, SQL text, or internal paths into the detail field for 5xx errors.
antipattern.reason: Exposes implementation details, internal hostnames, and sometimes credentials to external clients. Use a static detail string and provide traceId for internal lookup.

antipattern.description: Returning Content-Type: application/json instead of application/problem+json.
antipattern.reason: Clients that detect the +json media type suffix (e.g., Spring ProblemDetail) fall back to generic parsing. RFC 7807 mandates the problem+json type; the methodology's consistency guarantee depends on it.

antipattern.description: HTTP status code on the response does not match the status field in the body.
antipattern.reason: RFC 7807 requires both to match. Mismatch appears when wrapping downstream errors: the HTTP envelope says 502 while the body says 400, confusing clients and monitoring tools.

antipattern.description: Using errors[] for non-validation error types (401, 403, 404, 429).
antipattern.reason: errors[] is a field-level extension for validation failures. Single-cause errors carry all necessary information in detail. Adding an empty errors[] array confuses clients expecting the spec shape.

antipattern.description: type field contains a non-URI string such as "validation" or "not_found".
antipattern.reason: RFC 7807 requires type to be a URI. Non-URI values are technically non-conforming. More importantly, they cannot link to documentation pages that explain the error, recovery steps, and errors[] shape.

antipattern.description: Skipping traceId on successful error handling (only populating it on 500).
antipattern.reason: Tracing 400-class errors is equally important for debugging client-integration issues. Always populate traceId from W3C traceparent or generate a UUID.

antipattern.description: errors[].code contains a translated or localized string.
antipattern.reason: code is machine-readable and must be stable. Translate errors[].message for users, but keep code as an invariant slug (email_invalid, not "Email invalide").

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: For each endpoint: (1) define a small set of error `type` slugs (e.g., `validation-error`, `not-found`, `unauthorized`, `rate-limited`, `internal-error`), (2) host a docs page per slug at `/errors/[slug]`, (3) implement a framework-level exception handler that returns Problem Details with `Content-Type: application/problem+json`, (4) propagate `traceparent` from request → response, (5) add a contract test asserting the error shape on each error class. Validate via `schemathesis` or a custom Pydantic/Zod schema check in CI.

Recommended subagents: faion-api-developer owns the contract definition and per-error documentation pages (draft the type URIs, error slugs, and the semantic meaning of each); faion-code-agent implements per-framework exception handlers (DRF custom handler, FastAPI RequestValidationError override, Express middleware, gin handler); faion-test-agent writes contract tests asserting Problem Details shape and headers; faion-software-architect decides which errors are exposed vs suppressed (5xx detail leaks, PII masking, etc.); faion-sdd-execution drives the rollout as a feature with a backwards-compat phase.

section.name: limitations-and-tradeoffs
section.p: README is schema-only: no language-specific implementation, no integration with FastAPI/DRF/Express/Gin. traceId is shown but no guidance on how to populate it (W3C traceparent header, OTel context, etc.). errors[] is a custom extension to RFC 7807 — clients written to the spec may not parse it. Document explicitly. type is supposed to be a URL pointing to docs; without that infra (/errors/[slug] page hosted), it's a dead string. No anti-fingerprint guidance — detail should not leak stack traces, internal paths, SQL errors, or PII. instance is sometimes a path, sometimes a request ID — RFC says URI of the specific occurrence; agents pick whichever. Doesn't cover localization (title in user's language vs always English). No content-type discussion (application/problem+json is the spec; application/json is technically wrong).

list.item: Define error type URIs at the API design stage, not retroactively. The type is part of the contract.
Document each error type in a dedicated page at `/errors/[slug]` with recovery steps, example causes, and expected action by the client.
Use consistent field naming across all frameworks (not `detail` vs `description` vs `message`).
Propagate traceId through distributed traces using W3C traceparent format.
Test the error shape in contract tests, not just happy path.
Version the Problem Details format (X-Problem-Details-Version header) if you need to evolve it.
Log the full exception server-side; return only traceId and generic detail to the client.

section.name: cli-tools-and-validation
section.p: schemathesis — property tests against OpenAPI; checks error responses match spec. dredd — API contract testing against OpenAPI/Blueprint. oasdiff — detect breaking changes including error response shape. httpie / curl -i — manual probe; inspect Content-Type and body structure. jq — assert JSON shape in shell and parse error chains. ajv (CLI) — JSON Schema validation against captured response. bunyan / pino (Node) + slog (Go) + structlog (Python) — structured error logging matching traceId for correlating errors with spans. otel-cli — inject + propagate W3C traceparent in tests and trace production errors.

template.purpose: JSON Schema 2020-12 for validating Problem Details responses in CI
